# UC-10.12.16 · ePHI Access Audit

## Description

HIPAA Security Rule requires accounting of disclosures; supports minimum necessary and investigation workflows.

## Value

HIPAA Security Rule requires accounting of disclosures; supports minimum necessary and investigation workflows.

## Implementation

De-identify MRNs in dashboards where possible; alert on bulk export and after-hours access to VIP records.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: EHR audit TA, Active Directory.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype="epic:audit"`, `sourcetype="cerner:audit"`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
De-identify MRNs in dashboards where possible; alert on bulk export and after-hours access to VIP records.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=healthcare sourcetype IN ("epic:audit","cerner:audit") object_type="chart"
| search action IN ("VIEW","EXPORT","PRINT")
| stats count, dc(patient_mrn) as patients by user_id, department
| where patients>50 OR count>200
| sort -patients
```

Understanding this SPL

**ePHI Access Audit** — HIPAA Security Rule requires accounting of disclosures; supports minimum necessary and investigation workflows.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype="epic:audit"`, `sourcetype="cerner:audit"`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): EHR audit TA, Active Directory. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: healthcare.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=healthcare. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by user_id, department** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where patients>50 OR count>200` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search on recent production data, spot-check a handful of results against the source system or operational runbooks (cash operations, EHR, payment gateway, etc.), and adjust thresholds to match your seasonal patterns and test windows so teams are not flooded with noise.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (high-volume accessors), Heatmap (user × department), Timeline (access bursts).

## SPL

```spl
index=healthcare sourcetype IN ("epic:audit","cerner:audit") object_type="chart"
| search action IN ("VIEW","EXPORT","PRINT")
| stats count, dc(patient_mrn) as patients by user_id, department
| where patients>50 OR count>200
| sort -patients
```

## Visualization

Table (high-volume accessors), Heatmap (user × department), Timeline (access bursts).
